records.ety: [AS. hnītan to strike against, imp. hnāt.]

defs: To butt; to push with the horns.

records.ety: [AS. nāt; ne not + wāt wot. See Not, and Wot.]

defs: Know not; knows not.
Nut.
Need; needful business.

records.ety: [F. note, L. nota; akin to noscere, notum, to know. See Know.]

defs: A mark or token by which a thing may be known; a visible sign; a character; a distinctive mark or feature; a characteristic quality.
quotes: Whosoever appertain to the visible body of the church, they have also the notes of external profession.
She [the Anglican church] has the note of possession, the note of freedom from party titles,the note of life -- a tough life and a vigorous.
What a note of youth, of imagination, of impulsive eagerness, there was through it all !

defs: A mark, or sign, made to call attention, to point out something to notice, or the like; a sign, or token, proving or giving evidence.
A brief remark; a marginal comment or explanation; hence, an annotation on a text or author; a comment; a critical, explanatory, or illustrative observation.
quotes: The best writers have been perplexed with notes, and obscured with illustrations.

defs: A brief writing intended to assist the memory; a memorandum; a minute.

Hence, a writing intended to be used in speaking; memoranda to assist a speaker, being either a synopsis, or the full text of what is to be said; as, to preach from notes; also, a reporter's memoranda; the original report of a speech or of proceedings.
A short informal letter; a billet.
A diplomatic missive or written communication.
A written or printed paper acknowledging a debt, and promising payment; as, a promissory note; a note of hand; a negotiable note.
A list of items or of charges; an account.
quotes: Here is now the smith's note for shoeing.

defs: A character, variously formed, to indicate the length of a tone, and variously placed upon the staff to indicate its pitch. Hence:
A musical sound; a tone; an utterance; a tune.
A key of the piano or organ.
quotes: The wakeful bird . . . tunes her nocturnal note.
That note of revolt against the eighteenth century, which we detect in Goethe, was struck by Winckelmann.

defs: Observation; notice; heed.
quotes: Give orders to my servants that they take No note at all of our being absent hence.

defs: Notification; information; intelligence.
quotes: The king . . . shall have note of this.

defs: State of being under observation.
quotes: Small matters . . . continually in use and in note.

defs: Reputation; distinction; as, a poet of note.
quotes: There was scarce a family of note which had not poured out its blood on the field or the scaffold.

defs: Stigma; brand; reproach.

records.ety: [F. noter, L. notare, fr. nota. See Note, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Noted; p. pr. & vb. n. Noting.]

defs: To notice with care; to observe; to remark; to heed; to attend to.
quotes: No more of that; I have noted it well.
The world will little note, nor long remember, what we say here, but it can never forget what they did here.

defs: To record in writing; to make a memorandum of.
quotes: Every unguarded word . . . was noted down.

defs: To charge, as with crime (with of or for before the thing charged); to brand.
quotes: They were both noted of incontinency.

defs: To denote; to designate.
To annotate.
To set down in musical characters.
